section.title: Hagar and Sarah
section.sectionRationale: Paul closes his doctrinal argument with an illustration drawn from Abraham's two sons. Hagar the slave woman and Sarah the free woman stand for two covenants: one bearing children into slavery, tied to the present Jerusalem, and one that is free, the Jerusalem above, our mother. As Isaac was a child of promise born by the Spirit, so are believers, and Scripture says to expel the slave woman, for believers are children of the free.
section.thesis: The story of Hagar and Sarah pictures two covenants, one of slavery under law and one of freedom by promise; believers, like Isaac, are children of promise born according to the Spirit, and belong to the free woman and the heavenly Jerusalem, so they must not submit to the slavery of the law.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the two sons of Abraham, one by the slave woman and one by the free; the two covenants they represent, Sinai and slavery versus the Jerusalem above and freedom; and the conclusion, believers are children of promise and of the free woman, not the slave.

units.label: Abraham's two sons
units.note: one by the slave woman, one by the free

verses.text: Tell me, you who want to be under the law, do you not understand what the law says?
For it is written that Abraham had two sons, one by the slave woman and the other by the free woman.
His son by the slave woman was born according to the flesh, but his son by the free woman was born through the {{epaggelia|promise}}.

units.label: Two covenants
units.note: Sinai and slavery, the Jerusalem above and freedom

verses.text: These things serve as {{allegoreo|illustrations}}, for the women represent two covenants. One covenant is from Mount Sinai and bears children into slavery: This is Hagar.
Now Hagar stands for Mount Sinai in Arabia and corresponds to the present-day Jerusalem, because she is in slavery with her children.
But the Jerusalem above is {{eleuthera|free}}, and she is our mother.

For it is written: “Rejoice, O barren woman, who bears no children; break forth and cry aloud, you who have never travailed; because more are the children of the desolate woman than of her who has a husband.”

units.label: Children of the free woman
units.note: born by the Spirit, heirs of freedom

verses.text: Now you, brothers, like Isaac, are children of promise.
At that time, however, the son born by the flesh persecuted the son born by the Spirit. It is the same now.
But what does the Scripture say? “Expel the slave woman and her son, for the slave woman's son will never share in the inheritance with the free woman's son.”
Therefore, brothers, we are not children of the slave woman, but of the free woman.

epaggelia.gloss: A promise, a pledge freely given. Isaac was born through the promise, not by human contrivance like Ishmael, who was born according to the flesh. The word marks the whole way of grace: God pledges, and He performs, so that the child of promise owes his existence to God's word and power, not to human effort. Believers are children of promise in the same way.

allegoreo.gloss: To speak allegorically, to use a historical account as an illustration of a deeper truth. Paul is not denying that Hagar and Sarah were real people in real history; he draws out the pattern God built into the story, so that the two women picture two covenants. The account teaches by correspondence, slavery and freedom, flesh and promise.

eleuthera.gloss: Free, the free woman, contrasted with the slave. Sarah is the free woman, and the Jerusalem above is free, and she is the mother of all who belong to Christ. Freedom is the birthright of the children of promise, over against the slavery of those who seek acceptance by the law. It is the same freedom Paul will urge them to stand firm in.

basic.context: Paul makes his final argument to those who want to be under the law, using a story from their own Scriptures. Abraham had two sons: Ishmael, born the ordinary way to Hagar the slave, and Isaac, born by God's promise to Sarah the free woman. Paul says these two women picture two covenants, slavery under law and freedom by promise. Believers are like Isaac, children of promise, so they belong to freedom.
basic.hermeneutics: Paul reads the Old Testament story as a God given picture. He is not making up meaning but drawing out the pattern of slavery and freedom, flesh and promise, that the account contains. Read it as Paul teaching the Galatians to see themselves in Isaac and to refuse the slavery the story warns against.
basic.originalLanguages: Isaac was born through the epaggelia, the promise, not by human effort. Paul calls the story an allegoreo, an illustration that pictures two covenants. Sarah is the eleuthera, the free woman, and the Jerusalem above is free, the mother of all believers.
basic.typology: Isaac, the child of promise born by God's power, is the pattern of every believer born by the Spirit. Sarah and the heavenly Jerusalem picture the covenant of grace. The persecution of Isaac by Ishmael foreshadows the way children of the flesh oppose children of the Spirit, then and now.
basic.christ: The freedom Paul celebrates is the freedom Christ secured. The Jerusalem above, the city of the free, is the community gathered around the risen Christ. Believers are children of promise only because they belong to Christ, the true seed, in whom the promise to Abraham comes true.
basic.god: God is the promise keeper who gave Isaac by His own power and gives new birth by His Spirit. He is the God of the heavenly Jerusalem, gathering a free people. He fulfills the ancient words of Isaiah about the barren woman rejoicing, bringing many children to the once desolate by grace.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 21 tells of Isaac, Ishmael, and the command to send away the slave woman. Isaiah 54 is the song of the barren woman rejoicing that Paul quotes. Hebrews 12 speaks of the heavenly Jerusalem believers have come to.
basic.oneStory: The story of Abraham's household becomes a picture of the whole Bible: slavery under law versus freedom by promise. Believers are children of promise, born by the Spirit, citizens of the Jerusalem above. The gospel gathers a free family, fulfilling Isaiah's vision of the barren woman with countless children.

context.literary: This is the last of Paul's arguments in the doctrinal section, an extended illustration from Genesis capped by two Scripture quotations, Isaiah 54:1 and Genesis 21:10. The paired contrasts, slave and free, flesh and promise, Sinai and the Jerusalem above, structure the whole and drive to the concluding declaration in verse 31.
context.historical: The agitators appealed to descent from Abraham and to the law of Moses to press their case. Paul turns the family story of Abraham against them: the true children of Abraham are those born of promise and the Spirit, not those who claim the law. The persecution of the free by the children of the flesh matched the Galatians' own experience of pressure.
context.canonical: The passage models a Christ centered reading of the Old Testament, drawing out the pattern God built into Israel's history. It joins Isaiah's promise of a fruitful barren woman and the Genesis narrative into a single testimony that the covenant of grace brings freedom and a countless family.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show from Israel's own Scriptures that there are two covenants, slavery under law and freedom by promise, and that believers, like Isaac, are children of promise born by the Spirit, so they must not submit to the slavery of the law.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative of Hagar and Sarah is described and interpreted; the prescriptive conclusion is the command of Scripture applied in verses 30 and 31, to reject the way of slavery and live as children of the free woman.

debates.question: Is Paul denying the history of Hagar and Sarah by calling it an allegory?

views.label: Real history with a God given pattern
views.case: Paul treats the events as real but draws out the correspondence built into them; the account teaches by illustration without ceasing to be true history.

views.label: A free allegorizing of the text
views.case: Some read Paul as imposing meaning, but his point rests on the actual contrast in Genesis between the son of the slave and the son of promise.

debates.bearing: Paul reads the Old Testament as pointing to the gospel by the pattern God set within real history; the story genuinely warns against the slavery of the law.

debates.question: What is the persecution of the son born by the Spirit?

views.label: Pressure from those who trust the flesh
views.case: As Ishmael mocked Isaac, so those who rely on law and flesh oppose those who live by promise and Spirit, which the Galatians were experiencing from the agitators.

views.label: General hostility to the gospel of grace
views.case: Some broaden it to the world's opposition to grace, but the immediate reference is the pressure of the law party on the free children of promise.

debates.bearing: The gospel of freedom often provokes opposition from religion that trusts the flesh; believers should expect it and stand firm as children of the free woman.

study.originalLanguages: The engine of the passage is epaggelia, promise: Isaac was born through God's promise, not human striving, and believers likewise are children of promise. Paul calls the account an allegoreo, an illustration that carries a deeper correspondence, so the two women picture two covenants without ceasing to be real history. And the goal word is eleuthera, free: Sarah is the free woman, and the Jerusalem above is free, our mother. The whole argument sets slavery under the law against the freedom of grace, and lands the Galatians firmly among the free.

typology.fulfillment: Isaac, born by God's power and promise, patterns every believer born of the Spirit, a child of promise rather than of the flesh.
The free woman and the heavenly city picture the covenant of grace and the free family God gathers in Christ.
Isaiah 54:1 is fulfilled as the gospel brings a countless family to the once desolate, the mother of all who believe.

study.christ: The freedom Paul proclaims is the freedom Christ won. The Jerusalem above, the city of the free, is the people gathered around the risen and reigning Christ, and believers are children of promise only because they are joined to Him, the true seed of Abraham in whom the promise comes true. As Isaac lived by God's promise, so believers live by Christ, and the inheritance belongs to the children of the free woman who are His.
study.god: God is the faithful promise keeper who gave Isaac by His own power when nature could not, and who now gives new birth by His Spirit. He is the God of the heavenly Jerusalem, gathering a free people to Himself. He fulfills the ancient song of Isaiah, turning the barren and desolate into a mother of a countless family. His grace, not human effort, brings the children of promise into being.

crossReferences.note: The birth of Isaac and the command to send away Hagar and Ishmael, the source of Paul's illustration.
The barren woman told to rejoice for her many children, quoted in verse 27.
Believers have come to the heavenly Jerusalem, the city of the living God.
Not all Abraham's physical children are counted; the children of promise are his true offspring.

study.oneStory: The household of Abraham becomes a window onto the whole Bible: two ways, slavery under law and freedom by promise. Believers are children of promise, born by the Spirit and belonging to the Jerusalem above. The gospel gathers a free and countless family, fulfilling Isaiah's vision of the barren woman with more children than she could count, all of it the fruit of God's promise kept in Christ.

sermon.title: Sons of Freedom (Galatians 4:21 to 31)
sermon.why: Begg unpacks the illustration of Hagar and Sarah as two covenants, showing that believers are children of promise born by the Spirit, citizens of the free Jerusalem above, and must not return to the slavery of the law.

quiz.q: What do Hagar and Sarah represent in Paul's illustration?

options.text: Two covenants, one of slavery under law and one of freedom by promise.
options.note: Verse 24 states the women represent two covenants.

options.text: Two rival nations at war with each other.
options.note: They picture covenants, not merely warring nations.

options.text: Two equally good ways to reach God.
options.note: One leads to slavery, the other to freedom; they are not equal.

options.text: Two stages every believer must pass through.
options.note: They are contrasting covenants, not sequential stages.

quiz.q: How was Isaac, the son of the free woman, born?

options.text: Through God's promise, not according to the flesh.
options.note: Verse 23 contrasts promise with flesh.

options.text: By ordinary human planning like Ishmael.
options.note: Ishmael was born according to the flesh; Isaac through promise.

options.text: By Abraham's obedience to the law.
options.note: Isaac was born by promise, long before the law was given.

options.text: By Sarah's own strength and youth.
options.note: Sarah was barren; Isaac came by God's promise and power.

quiz.q: To which mother do believers belong?

options.text: To the free woman and the Jerusalem above.
options.note: Verses 26 and 31 place believers among the free.

options.text: To Hagar and the present Jerusalem in slavery.
options.note: That is the covenant of slavery believers do not belong to.

options.text: To both mothers equally.
options.note: Paul says clearly we are children of the free woman, not the slave.

options.text: To neither; they have no mother in the story.
options.note: Believers are children of the free woman, the heavenly Jerusalem.

quiz.q: What does the quotation from Isaiah celebrate?

options.text: The once barren woman rejoicing over a countless family.
options.note: Verse 27 quotes Isaiah 54:1 about the desolate becoming fruitful.

options.text: The destruction of Jerusalem.
options.note: The song is about joyful fruitfulness, not destruction.

options.text: The triumph of the slave woman.
options.note: It celebrates the free and formerly barren woman, Sarah's line.

options.text: The end of all promises.
options.note: It celebrates the fulfillment of promise, a countless family.

quiz.q: What does Scripture command regarding the slave woman?

options.text: Expel the slave woman, for her son will not share the inheritance with the free son.
options.note: Verse 30 quotes Genesis 21:10 to exclude the way of slavery.

options.text: Keep both sons as equal heirs.
options.note: Scripture says the slave woman's son will not share the inheritance.

options.text: Give the inheritance to the slave woman's son.
options.note: The inheritance belongs to the free woman's son.

options.text: Ignore the whole matter of inheritance.
options.note: The inheritance is precisely the point; it goes to the free.

questions: Believers are children of promise, born by the Spirit and not by human effort. How does knowing your new life is God's gift, not your achievement, free you from striving?
Paul says the children of the flesh persecute the children of the Spirit. Have you experienced pressure or ridicule for resting in grace rather than performance?
The Jerusalem above is free, and she is our mother. How does belonging to a heavenly city shape the way you hold the pressures of this present life?
Scripture calls the Galatians to refuse the way of slavery. What subtle forms of religious slavery do you need to send away?
Isaiah's barren woman rejoices over a countless family. How does the surprising fruitfulness of grace encourage you when your own efforts feel barren?

translationNotes.note: Illustrations renders a form of allegoreo; Paul draws out the God given pattern in the real history of Hagar and Sarah, not a denial that they lived.
Hagar corresponds to the present Jerusalem in slavery; the point is the covenant of law, not a comment on the geography alone.
The BSB footnote notes some manuscripts read we rather than you; either way believers are counted, like Isaac, as children of promise.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Galatians 4:21-31. No em dashes in this passage required substitution; verse 27 is set as poetry in the BSB and rendered here as continuous text with the wording unchanged. Sermon verified this session from the Truth For Life series A Study in Galatians, Volume 1 by Alistair Begg.
